content.intro: Not all data is the same. Understanding what kind of data you're working with is the first step in knowing how to analyse it.

blocks.content: Data comes in two broad categories: quantitative and qualitative. Understanding the difference shapes every decision you make — how you visualise, how you aggregate, how you draw conclusions.

left.label: Quantitative
left.description: Data expressed as numbers. Can be measured, added, averaged.

right.label: Qualitative
right.description: Data expressed as categories or descriptions. Describes qualities, not quantities.

blocks.content: Most datasets contain both. A customer record might have an ID (quantitative), a name (qualitative), a sign-up date (quantitative), and a preferred language (qualitative).
Quantitative data splits further into two types:

items.label: Discrete
items.detail: Countable whole numbers. You can have 3 customers, not 3.5 customers.

items.label: Continuous
items.detail: Measurable values on a scale. Revenue can be £42,317.83 — any value is valid.

blocks.question: A dataset records the following for each customer: age, country, number of purchases, and satisfaction rating (1–5). Which of these is qualitative?
blocks.explanation: Country is a category — it describes a quality, not a quantity. Age, purchases, and satisfaction rating are all numeric and quantitative.
